metadata.title: NVX-0228: A Novel BRD4 Inhibitor with Potent Antitumor Activity

chunks.content: NVX-0228 is a novel small-molecule inhibitor of bromodomain-containing protein 4 (BRD4) that demonstrates potent antitumor activity across multiple hematologic and solid tumor models. In this study, we report the discovery, optimization, and preclinical characterization of NVX-0228 as a selective BRD4-BD1 inhibitor with an IC50 of 12 nM in biochemical assays. The compound exhibits a molecular weight of 487.3 g/mol and a calculated LogP of 3.2, consistent with favorable drug-like properties. NVX-0228 showed significant tumor growth inhibition in xenograft models of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL).

Bromodomain and extra-terminal domain (BET) proteins, including BRD2, BRD3, BRD4, and BRDT, are epigenetic readers that recognize acetylated lysine residues on histones and other proteins. BRD4, in particular, has emerged as a critical therapeutic target in oncology due to its role in regulating MYC and other oncogenic transcription programs. Despite the clinical promise of pan-BET inhibitors such as JQ1 and OTX015, dose-limiting toxicities, particularly thrombocytopenia, have hampered their clinical development. We sought to develop a BD1-selective inhibitor to mitigate these on-target toxicities while preserving antitumor efficacy.

NVX-0228 was designed as a competitive inhibitor that binds directly to the acetyl-lysine recognition pocket of the BD1 bromodomain of BRD4. Structure-activity relationship (SAR) studies guided optimization from an initial hit compound (IC50 = 340 nM) through iterative medicinal chemistry to the final clinical candidate NVX-0228 (IC50 = 12 nM). The compound features a triazolopyridazine core scaffold with a pendant morpholine group that contributes to aqueous solubility. The binding mode was confirmed by co-crystallography, showing the compound occupying the acetyl-lysine binding pocket with key hydrogen bonds to Asn140 and the conserved water network.

Selectivity profiling demonstrated that NVX-0228 is BD1-selective with approximately 50-fold selectivity over BD2 (BD1 IC50 = 12 nM; BD2 IC50 = 600 nM). This selectivity profile was consistent across orthogonal assay formats including AlphaScreen, TR-FRET, and isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). Importantly, NVX-0228 showed minimal activity against BRD2 and BRD3 BD2 domains (IC50 > 1 μM), suggesting a favorable therapeutic window compared to pan-BET inhibitors.

In the Phase I dose-escalation trial (NCT05123456), NVX-0228 was administered orally to 48 patients with advanced hematologic malignancies refractory to at least two prior lines of therapy. The most common treatment-related adverse events were mild thrombocytopenia observed in 15% of subjects (all Grade 1-2), fatigue (21%), and nausea (12%). No dose-limiting toxicities were observed at doses up to 150 mg BID. The recommended Phase II dose (RP2D) was established at 200 mg BID.

Table 2. Clinical Response Data from Phase I Trial NCT05123456

Tumor Type | N | PR | SD | PD | ORR
AML | 12 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 33%
DLBCL | 10 | 2 | 4 | 4 | 20%
MM | 8 | 1 | 3 | 4 | 12.5%
NHL-other | 6 | 0 | 3 | 3 | 0%
MDS | 5 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 20%
CML | 4 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0%
ALL | 3 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0%

PR = partial response; SD = stable disease; PD = progressive disease; ORR = overall response rate. Responses assessed per IWG criteria (hematologic) or RECIST 1.1 (solid tumors). Median follow-up: 4.2 months.

Preliminary pharmacokinetic analysis from the Phase I trial revealed dose-proportional exposure across the evaluated dose range (25-200 mg BID). At the RP2D of 200 mg BID, the mean Cmax was 1,240 ng/mL (SD = 320) with a Tmax of 1.5 hours. The apparent terminal half-life was estimated at 5.8 hours based on limited sampling, suggesting twice-daily dosing is appropriate.

In summary, NVX-0228 represents a first-in-class BD1-selective BRD4 inhibitor with a competitive mechanism of action targeting the acetyl-lysine pocket. The compound demonstrates potent biochemical activity (IC50 = 12 nM), favorable selectivity (50-fold BD1/BD2), and preliminary evidence of clinical activity in AML (ORR 33%). The observed safety profile, with mild thrombocytopenia in only 15% of patients, compares favorably to pan-BET inhibitors and supports further clinical development. A Phase II expansion cohort in AML is planned.
